schools.summary: The lot of Manasseh, the firstborn of Joseph, and Machir's portion of Gilead: 'there was also a lot for the tribe of Manasseh; for he was the firstborn of Joseph; to wit, for Machir the firstborn of Manasseh, the father of Gilead: because he was a man of war, therefore he had Gilead and Bashan' — Manasseh, the firstborn of Joseph, had his lot, and Machir, the man of war, had Gilead and Bashan (the there was also a lot for the tribe of Manasseh; for he was the firstborn of Joseph, Manasseh the firstborn, the to wit, for Machir the firstborn of Manasseh, the father of Gilead: because he was a man of war, therefore he had Gilead and Bashan, Machir's portion, the figure, the lot of Manasseh the firstborn, and Machir the man of war given Gilead, the truth that Machir, the valiant, had won Gilead and Bashan east of Jordan, the figure of the valiant rewarded with the land he had taken, the man of war honoured, the lot of Manasseh, the portion given, vv.6); Manasseh, the firstborn of Joseph (the lot) — the firstborn (Manasseh); 'Machir the firstborn of Manasseh... because he was a man of war, therefore he had Gilead and Bashan' (Machir, the man of war, given Gilead, the reward, the figure, the valiant rewarded with the land he had taken, the providence, the lot, the rest of Manasseh's families to follow, vv.2) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: Zelophehad, who had no sons but five daughters: 'but Zelophehad, the son of Hepher, the son of Gilead, the son of Machir, the son of Manasseh, had no sons, but daughters: and these are the names of his daughters, Mahlah, and Noah, Hoglah, Milcah, and Tirzah' — Zelophehad died without sons, leaving five daughters (the but Zelophehad, the son of Hepher, the son of Gilead, the son of Machir, the son of Manasseh, had no sons, but daughters, no sons but daughters, the and these are the names of his daughters, Mahlah, and Noah, Hoglah, Milcah, and Tirzah, the five daughters named, the figure, the daughters of Zelophehad, who would claim their father's inheritance, the truth that Zelophehad left no son, but five daughters who sought their portion, the figure of the faith that claims the inheritance though no son remain, the daughters honoured, the daughters named, the claim to come, vv.2); Zelophehad leaving no sons but daughters (the daughters) — the daughters (no sons); 'the names of his daughters, Mahlah, and Noah, Hoglah, Milcah, and Tirzah' (the five daughters named, the heirs, the figure, the faith that claims the inheritance though no son remain, the providence, the daughters, their claim before Eleazar and Joshua to follow, vv.4) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The daughters of Zelophehad claiming and receiving their inheritance by the commandment of the LORD: 'and they came near before Eleazar the priest, and before Joshua the son of Nun, and before the princes, saying, The LORD commanded Moses to give us an inheritance among our brethren. Therefore according to the commandment of the LORD he gave them an inheritance among the brethren of their father' — the faith that claims the promise — 'and they came near before Eleazar the priest, and before Joshua... saying, The LORD commanded Moses to give us an inheritance among our brethren. Therefore according to the commandment of the LORD he gave them an inheritance among the brethren of their father': the daughters of Zelophehad came forward, pleading the word of the LORD given through Moses, and received their inheritance (the witnesses noting the significance — the holy boldness of the daughters who pleaded the promise of God and claimed their portion, and the faithfulness of God who gave it according to his word, the daughters resting their claim not on their own right but on the commandment of the LORD, 'the LORD commanded Moses,' and Joshua honouring the word and giving them the inheritance, the lesson — that faith claims the promise of God and pleads his word, and God is faithful to give what he has promised, the inheritance secured by his commandment, 'hath he said, and shall he not do it?' Numbers 23:19, the truth that the case of the daughters, decided by God through Moses, established a statute in Israel, the inheritance passing to daughters where there was no son, 'the daughters of Zelophehad speak right,' Numbers 27:7, the figure — the faith that pleads the promise and is not denied, the believer claiming the inheritance on the warrant of God's word, 'he is faithful that promised,' Hebrews 10:23, the truth that in Christ there is neither male nor female, the inheritance given to all who believe, 'if children, then heirs,' Romans 8:17; Galatians 3:28, the figure of the daughters, the weaker, made heirs by God's word, the faith that claims and possesses, the call to plead the promise of God in faith, the assurance that God gives the inheritance to all who claim it on his word, the figure of the faith that claims the promise of God and the justice of God that gives the inheritance to the believing); the daughters coming forward and pleading the commandment of the LORD given through Moses, resting their claim on his word (the faith that pleads the promise, Numbers 27:7) — the claim (the LORD commanded Moses); 'Therefore according to the commandment of the LORD he gave them an inheritance among the brethren of their father' (Joshua honouring the word of the LORD and giving the daughters their inheritance, God faithful to give what he promised, the inheritance given to the believing as in Christ the heirs are made, the inheritance secured, Numbers 23:19; Romans 8:17) — the figure (the faith that pleads the promise and is not denied, the providence, the inheritance, the ten portions of Manasseh to follow, vv.5-6) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the daughters of Zelophehad's claim and inheritance signify?

sides.position: The faith that claims the promise of God and the justice of God that gives the inheritance to the believing - the daughters pleading the word of the LORD given through Moses and resting their claim on his commandment, God faithful to give what he promised (Numbers 23:19; Numbers 27:7), a figure of the believer claiming the inheritance on the warrant of God's word (Hebrews 10:23), the heirs made in Christ where there is neither male nor female (Romans 8:17; Galatians 3:28)

Megiddo and her towns, even three countries' (the cities of Manasseh named, the strong places, the figure, the inheritance reaching among the brethren, the providence, the cities, the Canaanites of these cities not driven out to follow, vv.12-13) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: Manasseh's failure to drive out the Canaanites of those cities: 'yet the children of Manasseh could not drive out the inhabitants of those cities; but the Canaanites would dwell in that land' — Manasseh failed to drive out the Canaanites of those strong cities (the yet the children of Manasseh could not drive out the inhabitants of those cities, could not drive out, the but the Canaanites would dwell in that land, the Canaanites remaining, the figure, the failure to drive out the foe, the Canaanites left in the land, the truth that Manasseh, like Ephraim, left the Canaanites in their strong cities, the figure of the incomplete obedience that leaves the enemy, the world tolerated, 'they did not destroy the nations, concerning whom the LORD commanded them,' Psalm 106:34, the Canaanites left, the failure of full obedience, vv.10); Manasseh failing to drive out the Canaanites (the failure, Psalm 106:34) — the failure (could not drive out); 'the Canaanites would dwell in that land' (the Canaanites remaining in the land, the foe left, the figure, the incomplete obedience that leaves the enemy, the providence, the failure, the Canaanites put to tribute to follow, vv.13) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: Israel putting the Canaanites to tribute but not driving them out: 'yet it came to pass, when the children of Israel were waxen strong, that they put the Canaanites to tribute; but did not utterly drive them out' — when strong enough to expel the foe, Israel chose tribute instead of full obedience (the yet it came to pass, when the children of Israel were waxen strong, when Israel grew strong, the that they put the Canaanites to tribute; but did not utterly drive them out, tribute, not driven out, the figure, Israel, strong enough to drive out the foe, choosing gain over obedience, the truth that when they could have expelled the Canaanites, they spared them for tribute, the figure of the compromise that prefers gain to obedience, the foe tolerated for profit, 'covetousness, which is idolatry,' Colossians 3:5, the Canaanites spared for tribute, the half-hearted obedience, vv.12); Israel, grown strong, putting the foe to tribute (the gain, Colossians 3:5) — the tribute (when strong); 'did not utterly drive them out' (the foe not utterly driven out, the disobedience, the figure, the compromise that prefers gain to obedience, the providence, the failure, the complaint of the children of Joseph to follow, vv.14) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The complaint of the children of Joseph, that their lot was too small for so great a people: 'and the children of Joseph spake unto Joshua, saying, Why hast thou given me but one lot and one portion to inherit, seeing I am a great people, forasmuch as the LORD hath blessed me hitherto?' — the discontent of Joseph — 'and the children of Joseph spake unto Joshua, saying, Why hast thou given me but one lot and one portion to inherit, seeing I am a great people, forasmuch as the LORD hath blessed me hitherto?': the children of Joseph complained that their one lot was too small for so numerous a people (the witnesses noting the significance — the discontent of the proud and numerous tribe, pleading their greatness and God's blessing, yet unwilling to take the pains to possess what was given, the complaint mixing truth with pride, owning God's blessing in their increase yet murmuring against the portion, the slothful discontent that wants more without labour, the lesson — that men often plead their dignity and God's favour to excuse their own sloth, blaming their leaders for what their own want of faith and effort has left unpossessed, the murmuring that asks for more while neglecting to clear and possess the land already given, the truth that discontent and self-importance go ill together, the complaint of those who will not labour, 'the soul of the sluggard desireth, and hath nothing,' Proverbs 13:4, the figure — the believer who complains of his portion yet will not take the kingdom by the violence of faith and diligence, 'the kingdom of heaven suffereth violence, and the violent take it by force,' Matthew 11:12, the truth that God's blessing in increase is a call to greater labour, not an excuse for greater ease, the figure of the discontent that pleads its greatness yet shrinks from the cost, the call to possess the inheritance by faith and diligence rather than murmur, the warning against the pride that blames others for its own neglect, the figure of the discontent that pleads its greatness yet shrinks from the labour of possessing the inheritance); the children of Joseph complaining that their portion was too small, blaming Joshua for the narrowness of their lot (the discontent, Proverbs 13:4) — the complaint (but one lot); 'seeing I am a great people, forasmuch as the LORD hath blessed me hitherto?' (Joseph pleading their numbers and God's blessing, yet using it to murmur rather than to labour, the self-importance that excuses sloth, the kingdom not taken by those who will not strive, the discontent that pleads greatness, Matthew 11:12) — the figure (the believer who complains yet will not take the kingdom by violence, the providence, the complaint, Joshua's answer to clear the wood country to follow, vv.15) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: How is the complaint of the children of Joseph to be understood?

sides.position: The discontent that pleads its greatness yet shrinks from the labour of possessing the inheritance - the proud and numerous tribe pleading their numbers and God's blessing, yet unwilling to take the pains to possess what was given, the slothful desire that wants more without labour (Proverbs 13:4), men pleading their dignity to excuse their sloth, a figure of the believer who will not take the kingdom by the violence of faith and diligence (Matthew 11:12)

themes: And the children of Joseph spake unto Joshua, saying, Why hast thou given me but one lot and one portion to inherit, seeing I am a great people, forasmuch as the LORD hath blessed me hitherto?
the complaint of the children of Joseph, that their lot was too small (the crux of the discontent that pleads its greatness yet shrinks from the labour)
the discontent of the proud and numerous tribe, pleading their greatness and God's blessing, yet unwilling to take the pains to possess what was given
a figure of the believer who will not take the kingdom by the violence of faith and diligence, the murmuring that blames others for its own neglect (Proverbs 13:4; Matthew 11:12)

schools.summary: Joshua's answer, bidding the great people clear the forest and win room: 'and Joshua answered them, If thou be a great people, then get thee up to the wood country, and cut down for thyself there in the land of the Perizzites and of the giants, if mount Ephraim be too narrow for thee' — the reproof that bids them labour — 'and Joshua answered them, If thou be a great people, then get thee up to the wood country, and cut down for thyself there in the land of the Perizzites and of the giants, if mount Ephraim be too narrow for thee': Joshua retorted their own argument, bidding the great people prove their greatness by clearing the forest and possessing it (the witnesses noting the significance — the wise reproof that meets discontent not with indulgence but with a call to labour, turning their plea of greatness into a summons to exert it, Joshua not adding to their lot but bidding them make room by industry, clearing the wood country and driving out the Perizzites and the giants, the lesson — that the remedy for a narrow portion is diligence, not murmuring, that God gives room to those who labour and have faith, the great people to prove their greatness by their work, 'the hand of the diligent shall bear rule,' Proverbs 12:24, the truth that the slothful are answered not with more but with a call to use what they have, the inheritance enlarged by labour and faith, the figure — the kingdom won by striving, the believer bidden to lay hold of the promise by diligence and not to murmur, 'be not slothful, but followers of them who through faith and patience inherit the promises,' Hebrews 6:12, the truth that God's gifts are possessed by effort, the room made by those who will work and believe, the figure of the wise reproof that bids the discontented labour for what they desire, the call to win the inheritance by diligence rather than complaint, the assurance that the diligent shall have room, the figure of the wise reproof that bids the discontented labour for what they desire rather than murmur); Joshua retorting their own argument, bidding the great people clear the forest and make room by their labour (the reproof that bids them labour, Proverbs 12:24) — the answer (get thee up and cut down); 'there in the land of the Perizzites and of the giants, if mount Ephraim be too narrow for thee' (Joshua bidding them drive out the Perizzites and the giants and win room by faith and diligence, the inheritance enlarged by labour, the kingdom won by striving, the diligence that inherits, Hebrews 6:12) — the figure (the kingdom won by striving, the believer bidden to lay hold by diligence, the providence, the answer, Joseph's plea of the iron chariots to follow, vv.16) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does Joshua's answer to the children of Joseph teach?

sides.position: The wise reproof that bids the discontented labour for what they desire rather than murmur - turning their plea of greatness into a summons to exert it, the remedy for a narrow portion being diligence not murmuring, God giving room to those who labour and have faith (Proverbs 12:24), a figure of the kingdom won by striving and the promise inherited through faith and patience, not sloth (Hebrews 6:12)

themes: And Joshua answered them, If thou be a great people, then get thee up to the wood country, and cut down for thyself there in the land of the Perizzites and of the giants, if mount Ephraim be too narrow for thee
Joshua's answer, bidding the great people clear the forest and win room (the crux of the wise reproof that bids the discontented labour rather than murmur)
the wise reproof that meets discontent not with indulgence but with a call to labour, turning their plea of greatness into a summons to exert it
a figure of the kingdom won by striving, the believer bidden to inherit the promise through faith and patience and not sloth (Proverbs 12:24; Hebrews 6:12)

schools.summary: Joseph's plea that the hill was too small and the valley held iron chariots: 'and the children of Joseph said, The hill is not enough for us: and all the Canaanites that dwell in the land of the valley have chariots of iron, both they who are of Bethshean and her towns, and they who are of the valley of Jezreel' — Joseph pleaded that the hill was too narrow and the valley too strong for the iron chariots (the and the children of Joseph said, The hill is not enough for us, the hill too small, the and all the Canaanites that dwell in the land of the valley have chariots of iron, both they who are of Bethshean and her towns, and they who are of the valley of Jezreel, the iron chariots, the figure, Joseph pleading the strength of the foe, the iron chariots a terror, the truth that they shrank from the valley for fear of the chariots of iron, the figure of the unbelief that magnifies the obstacle and shrinks from the fight, the iron chariots an excuse, 'the people be strong... and the cities are walled, and very great,' Numbers 13:28, the chariots feared, the unbelief that draws back, vv.15); the hill pleaded too small (the complaint) — the plea (not enough); 'the Canaanites... have chariots of iron... and they who are of the valley of Jezreel' (the iron chariots feared, the obstacle magnified, the unbelief that shrinks, Numbers 13:28, the figure, the unbelief that magnifies the obstacle, the providence, the plea, Joshua's answer that they should drive them out to follow, vv.17-18) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And the children of Joseph said, The hill is not enough for us: and all the Canaanites that dwell in the land of the valley have chariots of iron, both they who are of Bethshean and her towns, and they who are of the valley of Jezreel
Joseph's plea that the hill was too small and the valley held iron chariots
Joseph pleading the hill too narrow and the valley too strong for the iron chariots
the unbelief that magnifies the obstacle and shrinks from the fight (Numbers 13:28)

schools.summary: Joshua granting that Joseph was great and should have more than one lot: 'and Joshua spake unto the house of Joseph, even to Ephraim and to Manasseh, saying, Thou art a great people, and hast great power: thou shalt not have one lot only' — Joshua granted their greatness and power and promised them more, but by their own effort (the and Joshua spake unto the house of Joseph, even to Ephraim and to Manasseh, saying, Thou art a great people, and hast great power, great and powerful, the thou shalt not have one lot only, not one lot only, the figure, Joshua owning their greatness and power, turning it to a call to use it, the truth that their very strength was the ground for bidding them win more, the figure of the gift that calls forth the effort, the strength to be used, 'to whom much is given... of him shall be much required,' Luke 12:48, the greatness owned, the call to use the power, vv.15); Joshua owning their greatness and power (the grant, Luke 12:48) — the greatness (great and powerful); 'thou shalt not have one lot only' (the promise of more than one lot, the call to use their power, the figure, the gift that calls forth the effort, the providence, the grant, the charge to clear the mountain and drive out the Canaanites to follow, vv.18) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And Joshua spake unto the house of Joseph, even to Ephraim and to Manasseh, saying, Thou art a great people, and hast great power: thou shalt not have one lot only
Joshua granting that Joseph was great and should have more than one lot
Joshua owning their greatness and power, promising them more than one lot
but turning their strength into a call to use it and win the inheritance (Luke 12:48)

schools.summary: Joshua's charge to clear the mountain and drive out the Canaanites despite their iron chariots: 'but the mountain shall be thine; for it is a wood, and thou shalt cut it down: and the outgoings of it shall be thine: for thou shalt drive out the Canaanites, though they have iron chariots, and though they be strong' — the faith that overcomes — 'but the mountain shall be thine; for it is a wood, and thou shalt cut it down... for thou shalt drive out the Canaanites, though they have iron chariots, and though they be strong': Joshua charged them to clear the mountain and to drive out the Canaanites despite the iron chariots they feared (the witnesses noting the significance — the assurance that the obstacle they magnified was no barrier to faith and diligence, the iron chariots no match for the God who gave the land, Joshua bidding them not to shrink from the chariots but to drive out the foe, the strength of the enemy no excuse where God has promised, the lesson — that faith and labour together possess the inheritance, the strongest foe overcome where God has given the word, the obstacle that unbelief magnifies vanishing before the courage that trusts God, 'if God be for us, who can be against us?' Romans 8:31, the truth that the chariots of iron, the terror of the valley, were nothing against the promise of God, the same God who burned the chariots at Merom giving the victory, 'some trust in chariots... but we will remember the name of the LORD,' Psalm 20:7, the figure — the faith and diligence that overcome the strongest obstacle, the believer bidden to lay hold of the promise and not to fear the power of the enemy, 'this is the victory that overcometh the world, even our faith,' 1 John 5:4, the truth that what God gives is won by labour and courage, the mountain cleared and the chariots driven out by faith, the figure of the faith that fears no chariot and possesses the promise, the call to drive out the foe in the strength of God, the assurance that the strong enemy yields to faith and diligence, the figure of the faith and diligence that overcome the strongest obstacle, the promise won by labour and courage); Joshua bidding them clear the wooded mountain and possess it, the inheritance won by their labour (the diligence that possesses, Psalm 20:7) — the charge (cut it down and possess); 'for thou shalt drive out the Canaanites, though they have iron chariots, and though they be strong' (Joshua assuring them they should drive out the Canaanites despite the iron chariots, the obstacle no barrier to faith, the strong foe overcome where God has promised, the faith that overcomes the world, Romans 8:31; 1 John 5:4) — the figure (the faith that fears no chariot and possesses the promise, the providence, the charge, the setting up of the tabernacle at Shiloh to follow, ch.18) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does Joshua's charge to drive out the Canaanites despite their iron chariots signify?

sides.position: The faith and diligence that overcome the strongest obstacle, the promise won by labour and courage - the iron chariots no barrier to faith and no match for the God who gave the land (Romans 8:31), the same God who burned the chariots at Merom giving the victory (Psalm 20:7), a figure of the faith that overcomes the world and fears no power of the enemy (1 John 5:4)

themes: But the mountain shall be thine; for it is a wood, and thou shalt cut it down: and the outgoings of it shall be thine: for thou shalt drive out the Canaanites, though they have iron chariots, and though they be strong
Joshua's charge to clear the mountain and drive out the Canaanites despite their iron chariots (the crux of the faith and diligence that overcome the strongest obstacle)
the assurance that the obstacle they magnified was no barrier to faith and diligence, the iron chariots no match for the God who gave the land
a figure of the faith that overcomes the world and fears no power of the enemy, the promise won by labour and courage (Romans 8:31; Psalm 20:7; 1 John 5:4)
